perturbation.original_text: The Evaluation Material will be used by Investor solely in connection with its evaluation of a possible Transaction, and not for any other purpose whatsoever. The Evaluation Material will be kept confidential by Investor in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement and will not be disclosed to any person, except that Investor may disclose the Evaluation Material or portions thereof to those of its employees, officers, directors, partners, members, affiliates, accountants, attorneys, agents, consultants and advisors (these persons being collectively called “ Representatives ”) who (a) need to know such information in order for Investor to be able to evaluate a possible Transaction and (b) have been directed by Investor to treat the Evaluation Material in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Evaluation Material will be used by Investor in connection with its evaluation of a possible Transaction, and not substantially for any other purpose. The Evaluation Material will be kept reasonably confidential by Investor, and disclosure to any person is permitted at Investor's discretion, except that Investor should attempt to limit disclosure of the Evaluation Material or portions thereof to its employees, officers, directors, partners, members, affiliates, accountants, attorneys, agents, consultants and advisors (these persons being collectively called “ Representatives ”) who (a) need to know such information in order for Investor to potentially evaluate a possible Transaction and (b) have been generally advised by Investor to treat the Evaluation Material with care.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity by replacing 'solely' with 'not substantially', 'kept confidential' with 'kept reasonably confidential', and stating disclosure is permitted 'at Investor's discretion' and replacing 'directed' with 'generally advised'. The phrase 'potentially evaluate' further weakens the obligation to confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Trade Secrets Act (765 ILCS 1065/2) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By weakening the confidentiality requirements and allowing disclosure at Investor's discretion, the modified text potentially fails to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by the Illinois Trade Secrets Act, making it harder to enforce the confidentiality of the Evaluation Material as a trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes Back to Act Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Printer-Friendly Version Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. ( ) 805 ILCS 35/1 (805 ILCS 35/1) (from Ch. 32, par. 1001) Sec. 1. This Act shall be known and may be cited as the Illinois Development Credit Corporation Act. (Source: Laws 1965, p. 577.) 805 ILCS 35/2 (805 ILCS 35/2) (from Ch. 32, par. 1002) Sec. 2. The purpose of this Act is to assist and encourage the development and advancement of the business prosperity and economic welfare of the State, to assist and encourage the expansion of existing industries and the location of new industries in this State, and to provide for maximum opportunities for employment, and to these ends to establish a source of credit not otherwise available through conventional commercial channels for the promotion, development and conduct of new and expanded business activities in this State. (Source: Laws 1965, p. 577.) 805 ILCS 35/3 (805 ILCS 35/3) (from Ch. 32, par. 1003) Sec. 3. As used in this Act, unless the context otherwise requires: "Development credit corporation" means any corporation organized pursuant to this Act for the purposes prescribed in Section 4 of this Act. "Financial institution" means any bank, trust company, savings and loan association, insurance company, or related corporation, partnership, foundation or other institution, authorized to do business in the State of Illinois and engaged primarily or largely in lending or investing funds. "Member" means any financial institution which undertakes to lend money to a development credit corporation upon its call and in accordance with Section 15 of this Act. "Board of Directors" means the board of directors of a development credit corporation. "Loan Limit" means, for any member, the maximum amount permitted to be outstanding at any one time on loans made by such member to a development credit corporation, as determined by the provisions of this Act and shall include the aggregate unpaid principal amount of all obligations or evidences of indebtedness, or participations therein, of any development credit corporation, owned by such member. "Director" means the Director of Financial Institutions. (Source: Laws 1965, p. 577.) 805 ILCS 35/4 (805 ILCS 35/4) (from Ch. 32, par. 1004) Sec. 4. The purposes of a development credit
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens confidentiality requirements, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard of the Illinois Trade Secrets Act. The original text ensures strict confidentiality, while the changed text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'solely' with 'not substantially', 'kept confidential' with 'kept reasonably confidential', permitting disclosure at 'Investor's discretion' and replacing 'directed' with 'generally advised'. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly mention trade secrets, the Law Explanation describes the potential violation of the Illinois Trade Secrets Act. The alterations significantly diminish the obligation to confidentiality. This undermines the enforceability of confidentiality, which can lead to legal issues related to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: At any time upon the written request of the Company, Investor will, at its own expense, (i) reasonably promptly redeliver to the Company or, at the at Investor’s option, destroy all copies (including permanently erasing or deleting any electronic copies) of the Evaluation Material in the possession or control of Investor or any of its Representatives and (ii) reasonably promptly destroy all analyses, notes, data, compilations, summaries, forecasts, studies or other documents and materials prepared by Investor or any of its Representatives in connection with their evaluation and review of a possible Transaction that contain, reflect or are based upon or generated from, in whole or in part, any of the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time upon the reasonable written request of the Company, Investor will, at its own expense, (i) try to redeliver to the Company or, at the Investor’s convenience, dispose of copies of the Evaluation Material if reasonably accessible and (ii) attempt to dispose of analyses, notes, data, compilations, summaries, forecasts, studies, and other documents related to the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity by adding 'reasonable' to the request, replacing 'reasonably promptly redeliver' with 'try to redeliver', 'at the Investor’s option' with 'at the Investor’s convenience', 'destroy' with 'dispose of', adding 'if reasonably accessible' and replacing 'reasonably promptly destroy' with 'attempt to dispose of'. The phrase 'related to' is less specific than the original text.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Replacing the mandatory destruction of Evaluation Material with a weaker obligation to 'try' or 'attempt' to dispose of the materials, only when reasonably accessible or at the Investor's convenience, undermines the protective measures necessary to safeguard trade secrets and contradicts the 'reasonable efforts' standard.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation to protect Evaluation Material, potentially contradicting the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection under laws like the Illinois Trade Secrets Act (as explained in the Law Explanation). The original text mandates 'reasonably promptly redeliver' or 'destroy', while the changed text uses softer language like 'try to redeliver' or 'attempt to dispose of', conditioned on 'reasonable accessibility' and the Investor's convenience. Although scraped snippet 1 doesn't directly mention trade secrets, the Law Explanation connects these changes to the reasonable efforts requirement for trade secret protection, making the weakening of the obligation a significant legal concern.
